title: Multiplicity One Conjecture in Min-max theory (continued)
speaker: Xin Zhou
affiliation: University of California, Santa Barbara; Member, School of Mathematics
abstract: I will present a proof with some substantial details of the Multiplicity One Conjecture in Min-max theory, raised by Marques and Neves. It says that in a closed manifold of dimension between 3 and 7 with a bumpy metric, the min-max minimal hypersurfaces associated with the volume spectrum introduced by Gromov, Guth, Marques-Neves are all two-sided and have multiplicity one.

proof_stages.label: Sweepouts, volume spectrum, and the conjecture
proof_stages.summary: The talk recalls the mod-2 cycle space, defines k-sweepouts via the cohomology generator, proves the induced fundamental-group map is surjective, reviews the k-volume spectrum and its minimal-hypersurface realization with index bound, and states the multiplicity-one conjecture for bumpy metrics.

proof_stages.label: Perturbative strategy via prescribed mean curvature
proof_stages.summary: The proof strategy is to replace the area functional by a Lagrange-multiplier prescribed-mean-curvature functional on domains and to split the argument into translating cycle min-max to domain min-max and then passing through the perturbed PMC theory.

proof_stages.label: Double cover by Caccioppoli sets and lifted sweepout
proof_stages.summary: The boundary map from Caccioppoli sets to mod-2 hypersurfaces is shown to be a two-to-one cover, and the surjectivity of the sweepout on fundamental groups is used to form a connected double cover of the parameter space on which the family lifts to domains.

proof_stages.label: Localization near the finite critical set and removal of fake peaks
proof_stages.summary: Possible min-max minimal hypersurfaces with bounded area and index form a finite set for a bumpy metric, a small contractible neighborhood defines the significant subset Y whose lift splits into two copies, and Pitts-type combinatorial tightening lowers high-area parts in the complement Z that do not approach such hypersurfaces.

proof_stages.label: Relative min-max class with a strict gap
proof_stages.summary: Fixing the lifted data over the complement Z, the remaining lifted peaks define a nontrivial relative homotopy class in the domain space whose min-max value is the original volume spectrum and is strictly larger than the maximum on the fixed part.

proof_stages.label: PMC min-max for the perturbed functional
proof_stages.summary: For small epsilon and a generic smooth function h, the relative min-max for area minus epsilon times the h-volume still has the strict gap and yields an almost-embedded boundary critical point with prescribed mean curvature epsilon h, multiplicity one at the perturbed level, and Morse index at most k.

proof_stages.label: Limit as the perturbation vanishes
proof_stages.summary: A Sharp-type compactness theorem for the PMC solutions gives a subsequential limit that is a smooth minimal hypersurface away from at most k concentration points, possibly with integer multiplicity m, satisfying m times its area equals the k-volume spectrum and with support index at most k.

proof_stages.label: Sheet analysis and the generic choice of h forcing m=1
proof_stages.summary: Using the boundary property to obtain alternating sheet orientations, the odd-multiplicity and well-separated even-multiplicity cases produce forbidden positive Jacobi fields, while the close even-multiplicity case would give a nonnegative solution of an inhomogeneous Jacobi equation; a generic h is chosen on the finite critical set so every such solution changes sign, yielding the desired contradiction.

examples.name: Willmore/Clifford-torus relative-homotopy analogy
examples.note: The proof of the Willmore conjecture is cited as a case where a contractible cycle space still carries a useful relative homotopy class detecting the Clifford torus, motivating the same relative strategy here.
examples.engagement_reason: The Willmore/Clifford-torus case is cited only as a motivating relative-homotopy analogy, with no construction or calculation carried out on it.

examples.name: Area graph with true and fake min-max peaks
examples.note: A schematic graph of area over the parameter space depicts genuine critical neighborhoods, fake peaks, and their lifted copies, making the localization and gap argument visible.
examples.engagement_reason: The speaker draws and reasons through the area graph, distinguishing genuine peaks from fake ones, defining the relevant regions, lifting peaks, and explaining how fake peaks are pulled down.

passes.reason: The fake high-area peaks are local min-max extrema illustrating that a peak need not detect a minimal hypersurface.

examples.name: Almost-embedded touching boundary picture
examples.note: A local touching configuration is used to illustrate what the PMC min-max regularity theorem allows while still producing a boundary with multiplicity one.
examples.engagement_reason: The almost-embedded touching picture is used only to illustrate what the PMC limit may look like and to state that it is still a boundary, without further analysis of that specific picture.

examples.name: Bump-up/bump-down perturbation forcing sheet intersection
examples.note: The pictured perturbation shows why a carefully chosen prescribed-curvature function makes two oppositely oriented sheets unable to stay too close, revealing the mechanism behind the key lemma.
examples.engagement_reason: The speaker works through the bump-up/bump-down perturbation picture, comparing top and bottom sheets and using the forced intersection to rule out the close-sheet case.

passes.reason: The bump-up/bump-down perturbation is an ordinary sign-changing perturbation used to obstruct the close even-sheet case by forcing intersection.
the sign-changing bump perturbation is chosen to obstruct the nonnegative height solution and force the close-sheet scenario to fail
the sign-changing bump perturbation is chosen as an obstruction to the nonnegative inhomogeneous Jacobi limit, forcing would-be close sheets to intersect
the sign-changing bump is an ordinary perturbation used as an obstruction because close mirrored sheets would intersect
The bump-up/bump-down perturbation is an ordinary sign-changing perturbation chosen adversarially to force close opposite sheets to intersect.

The bump-up/bump-down perturbation is an ordinary sign-changing test object used to obstruct close opposite sheets by forcing intersection.

examples.name: Three-minimal-surface finite critical set
examples.note: After an audience clarification about finiteness, the speaker sketches the choice of disjoint bump functions in the simplified case of three possible limiting minimal hypersurfaces.
examples.engagement_reason: The speaker explicitly runs the finite-critical-set construction for three minimal surfaces, choosing localized functions with disjoint supports and using them to build the required global perturbation.
